title: Pathways to Student Motivation: A Meta-Analysis of Antecedents of Autonomous and Controlled Motivations
description: Teacher autonomy support and need satisfaction were associated with self-determined motivation; competence was the strongest unique need-level predictor.
scope: 144 samples; 79,079 students; mean age 16.55
limitations: Correlational, highly heterogeneous, and not exclusively secondary-school evidence.
source_title: Pathways to Student Motivation: A Meta-Analysis of Antecedents of Autonomous and Controlled Motivations
source_author_or_org: Julien S. Bureau, Joshua L. Howard, Jane X. Y. Chong, Frédéric Guay
bias_or_access_issue: Broad educational levels and substantial cross-study heterogeneity limit KS3-KS4 specificity.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer causal effects or that competence alone is sufficient for adolescent motivation.

title: Student Motivation and Associated Outcomes: A Meta-Analysis From Self-Determination Theory
description: Intrinsic motivation related to success and well-being; identified regulation related strongly to persistence; external regulation showed limited adaptive associations.
scope: 344 samples; 223,209 students; 26 outcomes
limitations: Mostly observational evidence across broad ages and educational contexts.
source_title: Student Motivation and Associated Outcomes: A Meta-Analysis From Self-Determination Theory
source_author_or_org: Joshua L. Howard, Julien Bureau, Frédéric Guay, Jane X. Y. Chong, Richard M. Ryan
bias_or_access_issue: Broad age range, observational designs, and common-method measurement constrain causal interpretation.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that intrinsic motivation is always necessary or that controlled motives never support short-term action.

title: A Meta-Analytic Review of the Relationships Between Autonomy Support and Positive Learning Outcomes
description: Autonomy support correlated most strongly with autonomous motivation, engagement, and self-beliefs and least strongly with academic performance.
scope: 153 studies; 179 independent samples; 213,612 students
limitations: Wide heterogeneity, broad age range, perceived-support measures, and predominantly correlational designs.
source_title: A Meta-Analytic Review of the Relationships Between Autonomy Support and Positive Learning Outcomes
source_author_or_org: Sakhavat Mammadov, Kayla Schroeder
bias_or_access_issue: Perceived-support measures, broad educational levels, and gender moderation limit direct KS3-KS4 generalisation.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat autonomy support as a demonstrated direct cause of academic performance.

title: Effects of Need Supportive Teaching on Early Adolescents' Motivation and Engagement: A Review of the Literature
description: Student-perceived need-supportive teaching related positively to motivation and engagement.
scope: 71 studies of early adolescents
limitations: Observed or teacher-reported support produced smaller or null associations; causal evidence was limited.
source_title: Effects of Need Supportive Teaching on Early Adolescents' Motivation and Engagement: A Review of the Literature
source_author_or_org: Kim Stroet, Marie-Christine Opdenakker, Alexander Minnaert
bias_or_access_issue: Heavy reliance on student perceptions creates common-method risk; review predates recent secondary-school evidence.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not equate perceived support with observed teaching behaviour or established causality.

title: Self-Determination Theory Applied to Physical Education: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
description: Need support and autonomous motivation related to engagement and adaptive physical-education outcomes.
scope: School physical-education studies involving children and adolescents
limitations: Domain-specific, heterogeneous, and substantially reliant on correlational self-report evidence.
source_title: Self-Determination Theory Applied to Physical Education: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
source_author_or_org: Diego Vasconcellos et al.
bias_or_access_issue: Physical-education context and self-report measures limit transfer to other subjects and learning environments.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not generalise physical-education associations unchanged to all KS3-KS4 learning.

title: Self-Determined Motivation and Physical Activity in Children and Adolescents: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
description: Autonomous motivation showed moderate positive associations with physical activity; controlled motivation and amotivation showed weak negative associations.
scope: 46 studies; 15,984 children and adolescents
limitations: Mostly observational, heterogeneous, and limited to physical activity.
source_title: Self-Determined Motivation and Physical Activity in Children and Adolescents: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
source_author_or_org: Katherine B. Owen, Jordan Smith, David R. Lubans, Johan Y. Y. Ng, Chris Lonsdale
bias_or_access_issue: Physical-activity outcomes, heterogeneous measures, and methodological shortcomings constrain educational transfer.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat physical-activity motivation as equivalent to academic motivation or attainment.

title: Self-Determination Theory Based Instructional Interventions and Motivational Regulations in Organized Physical Activity: A Systematic Review and Multivariate Meta-Analysis
description: Interventions produced small improvements in intrinsic and identified motivation and small reductions in external regulation and amotivation.
scope: 38 intervention studies; 12,457 participants; weighted mean age 15.4
limitations: Heterogeneous effects; no effects for integrated or introjected regulation; physical-activity settings.
source_title: Self-Determination Theory Based Instructional Interventions and Motivational Regulations in Organized Physical Activity: A Systematic Review and Multivariate Meta-Analysis
source_author_or_org: Mika Manninen, Rod Dishman, Yongju Hwang, Eric Magrum, Yangyang Deng, Sami Yli-Piipari
bias_or_access_issue: Organised physical-activity settings and variation in study quality, duration, and comparators limit generalisation.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer large, uniform, or cross-subject intervention effects from small heterogeneous effects.

title: Effects of Out-of-School Physical Activity Interventions Based on Self-Determination Theory in Children and Adolescents: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
description: Pooled effects were nonsignificant for need satisfaction, motivation, amotivation, and physical activity.
scope: Nine youth intervention studies
limitations: Small evidence base, wide confidence intervals, and heterogeneous interventions.
source_title: Effects of Out-of-School Physical Activity Interventions Based on Self-Determination Theory in Children and Adolescents: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
source_author_or_org: Miguel Á. Tapia-Serrano et al.
bias_or_access_issue: Small English- and Spanish-language evidence base with out-of-school physical-activity focus.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim SDT-labelled youth interventions reliably change motivation or behaviour across settings.

title: A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation
description: Expected tangible contingent rewards reduced subsequent free-choice engagement on average; positive feedback increased interest and engagement.
scope: 128 studies across children, students, and adults
limitations: Older, laboratory-heavy evidence; free-choice engagement is not equivalent to learning.
source_title: A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation
source_author_or_org: Edward L. Deci, Richard Koestner, Richard M. Ryan
bias_or_access_issue: Older laboratory tasks and mixed-age samples limit ecological and KS3-KS4 validity.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that all rewards undermine learning, performance, or motivation in every context.

title: Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis
description: Intrinsic motivation predicted performance quality more strongly, while incentives predicted performance quantity more strongly.
scope: 183 samples; 212,468 participants across school, work, and physical domains
limitations: Broad domains and ages; much evidence was correlational and not adolescent-specific.
source_title: Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis
source_author_or_org: Christopher P. Cerasoli, Jessica M. Nicklin, Michael T. Ford
bias_or_access_issue: Cross-domain and adult-heavy evidence limits adolescent educational specificity.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat incentives and intrinsic motivation as inherently antagonistic or interchangeable across outcomes.

title: The Effects of Choice on Intrinsic Motivation and Related Outcomes: A Meta-Analysis of Research Findings
description: Choice generally produced small-to-moderate benefits for intrinsic motivation, effort, performance, and challenge preference.
scope: Experimental choice studies across children and adults
limitations: Large heterogeneity; effects depended on choice type, context, and participant characteristics.
source_title: The Effects of Choice on Intrinsic Motivation and Related Outcomes: A Meta-Analysis of Research Findings
source_author_or_org: Erika A. Patall, Harris Cooper, Jorgianne Civey Robinson
bias_or_access_issue: Mixed-age experimental literature and heterogeneous choice designs limit direct classroom application.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not equate autonomy with unrestricted choice or assume every choice improves motivation.

title: Motivation Interventions in Education: A Meta-Analytic Review
description: Theory-based motivation interventions showed positive educational effects overall.
scope: 74 reports; 92 effects; 38,377 students
limitations: Randomized effects were smaller than quasi-experimental effects; interventions and outcomes varied widely.
source_title: Motivation Interventions in Education: A Meta-Analytic Review
source_author_or_org: Rory A. Lazowski, Chris S. Hulleman
bias_or_access_issue: Intervention, outcome, age, and design heterogeneity; stronger quasi-experimental estimates raise bias concerns.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not apply the pooled effect to any specific intervention, learner group, or educational outcome.

title: Achievement Goals and Intrinsic Motivation: A Meta-Analytic Review
description: Performance goals generally reduced intrinsic motivation relative to mastery goals, conditional on feedback and approach-avoidance framing.
scope: Experimental achievement-goal studies
limitations: Older, often laboratory-based evidence using early achievement-goal classifications.
source_title: Achievement Goals and Intrinsic Motivation: A Meta-Analytic Review
source_author_or_org: Laird J. Rawsthorne, Andrew J. Elliot
bias_or_access_issue: Older experimental literature and early goal taxonomy may not represent contemporary secondary classrooms.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that all performance goals are harmful regardless of feedback, competence, or approach framing.

title: Achievement Goals and Self-Efficacy: A Meta-Analysis
description: Approach-versus-avoidance goal valence related meaningfully to self-efficacy; goal-definition distinctions were less consistent.
scope: 125 studies; 148 samples; 61,456 participants
limitations: Predominantly correlational, cross-sectional, and spread across educational ages.
source_title: Achievement Goals and Self-Efficacy: A Meta-Analysis
source_author_or_org: Chiungjung Huang
bias_or_access_issue: Cross-sectional designs, broad age coverage, and construct-measure variation limit causal inference.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that changing goal labels will directly or uniformly change self-efficacy.

title: The 3 × 2 Achievement Goals in the Education, Sport, and Occupation Literatures: A Systematic Review with Meta-Analysis
description: Task-, self-, and other-referenced approach and avoidance goals had distinct associations with motivation and performance.
scope: 56 articles; 58 samples; 35,031 participants from 15 countries
limitations: Domain mixing, correlational evidence, and uneven evidence across goal categories.
source_title: The 3 × 2 Achievement Goals in the Education, Sport, and Occupation Literatures: A Systematic Review with Meta-Analysis
source_author_or_org: Marc Lochbaum, Cassandra Sisneros, Zişan Kazak
bias_or_access_issue: Education, sport, and occupation samples are combined; country and category coverage is uneven.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not impose the 3 × 2 framework as a settled or universal account of adolescent goals.

title: I Believe, Therefore I Achieve (and Vice Versa): A Meta-Analytic Cross-Lagged Panel Analysis of Self-Efficacy and Academic Performance
description: Performance predicted later self-efficacy more strongly than self-efficacy predicted performance; efficacy-to-performance effects were unsupported in children.
scope: 11 longitudinal studies; 2,688 learners
limitations: Small, mostly White North American evidence base with limited school-age coverage.
source_title: I Believe, Therefore I Achieve (and Vice Versa): A Meta-Analytic Cross-Lagged Panel Analysis of Self-Efficacy and Academic Performance
source_author_or_org: Kate Talsma, Benjamin Schüz, Ralf Schwarzer, Kimberley Norris
source_type: Systematic review and meta-analytic cross-lagged analysis
bias_or_access_issue: Small, mostly White North American sample base and limited child/adolescent subgroup evidence.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim that confidence-building alone causes adolescent academic achievement.

title: Students' Achievement Values, Goal Orientations, and Interest: Definitions, Development, and Relations to Achievement Outcomes
description: Expectancies, values, goals, and interest are distinct influences on choice, persistence, and achievement and often decline across adolescence.
scope: Motivational development from childhood through adolescence
limitations: Narrative rather than meta-analytic; causal and cross-cultural evidence remained incomplete.
source_title: Students' Achievement Values, Goal Orientations, and Interest: Definitions, Development, and Relations to Achievement Outcomes
source_author_or_org: Allan Wigfield, Jenna Cambria
bias_or_access_issue: Narrative synthesis and incomplete cross-cultural coverage; developmental averages may obscure learner variation.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat adolescent motivational decline as universal, inevitable, or attributable to one cause.

title: To What Extent and Under Which Circumstances Are Growth Mind-Sets Important to Academic Achievement? Two Meta-Analyses
description: Overall associations and intervention effects were weak, with somewhat larger effects in selected lower-achieving or disadvantaged groups.
scope: Mindset-achievement associations and mindset interventions
limitations: Growth mindset is narrower than agency; intervention quality and implementation varied.
source_title: To What Extent and Under Which Circumstances Are Growth Mind-Sets Important to Academic Achievement? Two Meta-Analyses
source_author_or_org: Victoria F. Sisk, Alexander P. Burgoyne, Jingze Sun, Jennifer L. Butler, Brooke N. Macnamara
bias_or_access_issue: Intervention and sample heterogeneity; growth mindset is a narrow proxy for agency and motivation.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim broad or substantial achievement effects from mindset interventions.

title: A National Experiment Reveals Where a Growth Mindset Improves Achievement
description: A brief intervention produced small, context-dependent improvements among lower-achieving students.
scope: 12,490 US ninth-grade students across 65 public high schools
limitations: One country, age group, and intervention; average effects were small.
source_title: A National Experiment Reveals Where a Growth Mindset Improves Achievement
source_author_or_org: David S. Yeager et al.
bias_or_access_issue: US public-school sample; effects varied with school context and peer norms and may not transfer internationally.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not generalise a small context-dependent ninth-grade effect to all adolescents or motivational interventions.

title: Financial Incentives and Student Achievement: Evidence From Randomized Trials
description: Input-linked incentives sometimes changed behaviour; output-linked incentives generally did not reliably improve achievement.
scope: Approximately 38,000 US students across several cities
limitations: US programmes, short-run outcomes, and heterogeneous incentive designs.
source_title: Financial Incentives and Student Achievement: Evidence From Randomized Trials
source_author_or_org: Roland G. Fryer Jr.
bias_or_access_issue: US urban programmes, heterogeneous incentive structures, and short follow-up limit generalisation and durability claims.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that incentives reliably improve learning, durable motivation, or achievement across contexts.

title: The Attention System of the Human Brain: 20 Years After
description: Distinguishes alerting, orienting, and executive-control networks.
scope: General attention neuroscience
limitations: Limited direct educational and adolescent evidence.
source_title: The Attention System of the Human Brain: 20 Years After
source_author_or_org: Steven E. Petersen; Michael I. Posner
uncertainty_description: Educational relevance is inferred from general neuroscience.
bias_or_access_issue: Adult and laboratory evidence may not generalise to diverse KS3-KS4 learners or classroom contexts.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat attention as one fixed capacity or derive instructional prescriptions directly from neural architecture.

title: The Science of Mind Wandering: Empirically Navigating the Stream of Consciousness
description: Mind wandering can impair current performance but may support planning and generative thought.
scope: General and predominantly adult evidence
limitations: Variable definitions and heavy reliance on laboratory tasks.
source_title: The Science of Mind Wandering: Empirically Navigating the Stream of Consciousness
source_author_or_org: Jonathan Smallwood; Jonathan W. Schooler
uncertainty_description: The balance of costs and benefits in authentic adolescent learning remains uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: Predominantly adult samples and laboratory measures may underrepresent cultural, neurodevelopmental, and classroom variation.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not equate all mind wandering with disengagement, failure, or a deficit requiring suppression.

title: Developmental Changes in Cognitive Control Through Adolescence
description: Consistency and flexible deployment of cognitive control continue developing through adolescence.
scope: Adolescent development
limitations: Older narrative review; educational implications are indirect.
source_title: Developmental Changes in Cognitive Control Through Adolescence
source_author_or_org: Beatriz Luna
uncertainty_description: The extent and educational significance of developmental differences vary by task and context.
bias_or_access_issue: Neuroscience samples and tasks may not represent cultural, socioeconomic, neurodivergent, or classroom diversity.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that adolescents lack rationality, agency, or sophisticated cognitive control.

title: Adolescence as a Neurobiological Critical Period for the Development of Higher-Order Cognition
description: Adolescence may provide heightened plasticity for higher-order cognition.
scope: Adolescent developmental neuroscience
limitations: Critical-period framing remains provisional; classroom transfer is untested.
source_title: Adolescence as a Neurobiological Critical Period for the Development of Higher-Order Cognition
source_author_or_org: Bart Larsen; Beatriz Luna
uncertainty_description: The strength, timing, and educational meaning of any adolescent critical period remain uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: Neurobiological framing may obscure individual, cultural, environmental, disability, and access-related variation.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim a fixed adolescent critical-period boundary or use the review to prescribe a curriculum.

title: Executive Functions
description: Working memory, inhibition, and cognitive flexibility are distinguishable and context-sensitive.
scope: Developmental and general evidence
limitations: Broad narrative synthesis; some intervention evidence was preliminary.
source_title: Executive Functions
source_author_or_org: Adele Diamond
uncertainty_description: Construct boundaries, measurement, and intervention generalisability remain contested.
bias_or_access_issue: Broad evidence base may mask age, culture, disability, socioeconomic, and neurodivergent differences.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat executive function as a unitary trait or infer fixed learner capacity from task performance.

title: The Magical Mystery Four: How Is Working Memory Capacity Limited, and Why?
description: The focus of attention has limited capacity modified by chunking and knowledge.
scope: General working-memory evidence
limitations: Capacity estimates are task-dependent and not fixed person-level limits.
source_title: The Magical Mystery Four: How Is Working Memory Capacity Limited, and Why?
source_author_or_org: Nelson Cowan
uncertainty_description: Exact capacity estimates and their relevance to authentic adolescent learning remain uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: Laboratory paradigms may disadvantage learners through language, familiarity, disability, or culturally specific task demands.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not assign learners a fixed working-memory capacity or universal item limit.

title: Working Memory Skills and Educational Attainment: Evidence from National Curriculum Assessments at 7 and 14 Years of Age
description: Working-memory measures were associated with curriculum attainment.
scope: UK children and adolescents
limitations: Correlational and vulnerable to socioeconomic, language, and prior-knowledge confounding.
source_title: Working Memory Skills and Educational Attainment: Evidence from National Curriculum Assessments at 7 and 14 Years of Age
source_author_or_org: Susan E. Gathercole; Susan J. Pickering; Catherine Knight; Zoe Stegmann
uncertainty_description: Causal direction and the contribution of unmeasured contextual factors remain uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: UK assessment context, language demands, socioeconomic conditions, and test accessibility limit generalisability.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not interpret association as proof that working memory causes attainment or reflects innate ability.

title: Is Working Memory Training Effective? A Meta-Analytic Review
description: Training improves practised tasks, while durable far-transfer evidence is weak.
scope: Children and adults
limitations: Programmes, controls, and outcome measures vary.
source_title: Is Working Memory Training Effective? A Meta-Analytic Review
source_author_or_org: Monica Melby-Lervag; Charles Hulme
uncertainty_description: Durability and transfer may depend on intervention and outcome characteristics not consistently measured.
bias_or_access_issue: Included studies vary in age, access, disability representation, active controls, and publication context.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim working-memory training broadly raises intelligence, attainment, or general cognitive capacity.

title: Cognitive Training Does Not Enhance General Cognition
description: Broad cognitive-training benefits diminish under stronger controls and transfer tests.
scope: General, child, and adult training evidence
limitations: Aggregates heterogeneous interventions; domain-specific gains remain possible.
source_title: Cognitive Training Does Not Enhance General Cognition
source_author_or_org: Giovanni Sala; Fernand Gobet
uncertainty_description: Effects for specific populations, domains, and well-matched outcomes remain uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: Aggregated evidence may conceal age, disability, cultural, access, and intervention-quality differences.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not generalise narrow training gains to broad intelligence, cognition, or educational achievement.

title: Cognitive Architecture and Instructional Design: 20 Years Later
description: Novel processing is capacity-limited, while established knowledge reduces effective load.
scope: General cognitive-load and instructional evidence
limitations: Load measurement and some instructional interpretations remain contested.
source_title: Cognitive Architecture and Instructional Design: 20 Years Later
source_author_or_org: John Sweller; Jeroen J. G. van Merrienboer; Fred Paas
uncertainty_description: The measurement and separation of load types and their classroom application remain uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: General evidence may not capture linguistic, cultural, neurodivergent, disability, prior-knowledge, or resource differences.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that cognitive-load theory requires passive instruction or one method for all learners.

title: Expertise Reversal Effect and Its Implications for Learner-Tailored Instruction
description: Guidance useful to novices can become redundant or obstructive for knowledgeable learners.
scope: General learning and expertise
limitations: Expertise is domain-specific and frequently measured imperfectly.
source_title: Expertise Reversal Effect and Its Implications for Learner-Tailored Instruction
source_author_or_org: Slava Kalyuga
uncertainty_description: Thresholds at which guidance becomes redundant remain task- and learner-dependent.
bias_or_access_issue: Expertise classifications may reflect prior opportunity, language, access, and assessment familiarity rather than underlying capacity.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not label learners globally as novices or experts or remove support solely on age or attainment.

title: Domain-Specific Prior Knowledge and Learning: A Meta-Analysis
description: Domain-specific prior knowledge is positively associated with subsequent learning.
scope: Mixed ages and academic domains
limitations: Evidence is substantially correlational and uneven across ages and subjects.
source_title: Domain-Specific Prior Knowledge and Learning: A Meta-Analysis
source_author_or_org: Bianca A. Simonsmeier; Michaela Flaig; Anne Deiglmayr; Lennart Schalk; Michael Schneider
uncertainty_description: Causal mechanisms and generalisability across adolescent subjects and contexts remain uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: Prior knowledge measures may encode unequal educational opportunity, culture, language, and resource access.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not treat prior attainment or knowledge differences as innate capacity or learner worth.

title: Does Discovery-Based Instruction Enhance Learning?
description: Unaided discovery often underperforms, while scaffolded discovery can be effective.
scope: Children, adolescents, and adults
limitations: Intervention labels, controls, and outcomes are heterogeneous.
source_title: Does Discovery-Based Instruction Enhance Learning?
source_author_or_org: Louis Alfieri; Patricia J. Brooks; Naomi J. Aldrich; Harriet R. Tenenbaum
uncertainty_description: Which scaffolds work for which learners, domains, and goals remains uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: Aggregated studies may underrepresent cultural, disability, language, access, and classroom implementation differences.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim discovery is uniformly ineffective or that one instructional polarity is universally superior.

title: The Effect of Testing Versus Restudy on Retention: A Meta-Analytic Review of the Testing Effect
description: Retrieval practice generally improves delayed retention relative to restudy.
scope: Predominantly general and adult evidence
limitations: Many studies use adults, verbal materials, and researcher-designed tests.
source_title: The Effect of Testing Versus Restudy on Retention: A Meta-Analytic Review of the Testing Effect
source_author_or_org: Christopher A. Rowland
uncertainty_description: Generalisability to complex adolescent learning and authentic assessment remains uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: Predominantly adult, laboratory, verbal-material evidence may not represent diverse learners or accessible assessment conditions.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not equate retrieval practice with high-stakes testing or assume identical effects for complex learning.

title: Rethinking the Use of Tests: A Meta-Analysis of Practice Testing
description: Practice testing improves learning across settings, with important moderators.
scope: Mixed ages and educational settings
limitations: Studies vary in age, quality, stakes, feedback, and outcome alignment.
source_title: Rethinking the Use of Tests: A Meta-Analysis of Practice Testing
source_author_or_org: Olusola O. Adesope; Dominic A. Trevisan; Narayankripa Sundararajan
uncertainty_description: Effects in authentic KS3-KS4 contexts depend on stakes, feedback, material, and learner characteristics.
bias_or_access_issue: Testing formats may create language, anxiety, disability, cultural, and technology-access barriers.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that frequent high-stakes tests are beneficial or that all testing formats are equivalent.

title: Distributed Practice in Verbal Recall Tasks: A Review and Quantitative Synthesis
description: Spacing improves delayed retention; useful intervals depend partly on retention delay.
scope: General verbal-memory evidence
limitations: Predominantly verbal-recall tasks with limited authentic-school transfer evidence.
source_title: Distributed Practice in Verbal Recall Tasks: A Review and Quantitative Synthesis
source_author_or_org: Nicholas J. Cepeda; Harold Pashler; Edward Vul; John T. Wixted; Doug Rohrer
uncertainty_description: Optimal schedules for complex adolescent learning remain uncertain and outcome-dependent.
bias_or_access_issue: Laboratory verbal tasks may privilege language familiarity and may not represent disability, culture, subject, or classroom variation.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not prescribe one universal spacing interval or assume equivalent effects for all learning forms.

title: Using Spacing to Enhance Diverse Forms of Learning: Review of Recent Research and Implications for Instruction
description: Spacing benefits extend beyond simple word learning, although schedules vary by goal.
scope: Mixed ages, materials, and learning outcomes
limitations: Evidence is uneven for complex, authentic, long-duration outcomes.
source_title: Using Spacing to Enhance Diverse Forms of Learning: Review of Recent Research and Implications for Instruction
source_author_or_org: Shana K. Carpenter; Steven C. Pan; Andrew C. Butler
uncertainty_description: Durability and optimal spacing for complex KS3-KS4 outcomes remain uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: Mixed populations and tasks may conceal age, language, disability, cultural, technology, and implementation differences.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not impose a universal spacing schedule or claim equal benefits across subjects and outcomes.

title: Improving Students' Learning With Effective Learning Techniques: Promising Directions From Cognitive and Educational Psychology
description: Practice testing and spacing have broader support than highlighting or rereading.
scope: General and student learning evidence
limitations: Technique categories conceal implementation differences; much evidence is laboratory-based.
source_title: Improving Students' Learning With Effective Learning Techniques: Promising Directions From Cognitive and Educational Psychology
source_author_or_org: John Dunlosky; Katherine A. Rawson; Elizabeth J. Marsh; Mitchell J. Nathan; Daniel T. Willingham
uncertainty_description: Relative utility in diverse authentic adolescent contexts remains uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: Evidence may underrepresent disability, culture, language, socioeconomic access, subject differences, and classroom constraints.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not rank techniques as universally effective independent of material, learner, implementation, and outcome.

title: Effectiveness of Learning Strategy Instruction on Academic Performance: A Meta-Analysis
description: Learning-strategy instruction can improve attainment, with strong domain and measurement variation.
scope: Primary and secondary education
limitations: Effects were larger on researcher-developed tests; interventions were heterogeneous.
source_title: Effectiveness of Learning Strategy Instruction on Academic Performance: A Meta-Analysis
source_author_or_org: Anouk S. Donker; Hester de Boer; Danny Kostons; Charlotte C. Dignath van Ewijk; Margaretha P. C. van der Werf
uncertainty_description: Durability, independent transfer, and effects under external assessments remain uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: Interventions and measures may vary in language, culture, ability grouping, disability access, and researcher support.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not claim strategy instruction guarantees attainment or independent self-regulation across domains.

title: The Relation Between Self-Regulated Learning and Academic Achievement Across Childhood and Adolescence: A Meta-Analysis
description: Metacognitive processes and strategy use are positively associated with attainment.
scope: Children and adolescents
limitations: Substantial reliance on self-report and correlational designs limits causal inference.

title: The age of adolescence

prohibited_overextension: Do not attribute peer similarity solely to social pressure or infer uniform susceptibility.

title: The Teenage Brain: Peer Influences on Adolescent Decision Making
description: Peer presence can heighten attention to social rewards and alter adolescent decisions.
scope: Peer presence, reward sensitivity and adolescent decisions

source_author_or_org: Albert, D.; Chein, J.; Steinberg, L.
uncertainty_description: The extent to which laboratory peer-presence effects transfer to everyday relationships is uncertain.
bias_or_access_issue: Laboratory and predominantly Western evidence may privilege risk-focused interpretations.
downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not characterise adolescents as uniformly irrational or controlled by peers.

title: Peer Influence in Adolescence: Public-Health Implications for COVID-19
description: Peer influence can promote prosocial conduct as well as risk.

source_author_or_org: Andrews, J. L.; Foulkes, L.; Blakemore, S.-J.

downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that peer influence is inherently harmful or that pandemic behaviour generalises to all settings.

title: Peer Influence on Prosocial Behavior in Adolescence
description: Observed peer norms increased prosocial choices in an experimental task.

source_author_or_org: van Hoorn, J.; van Dijk, E.; Meuwese, R.; Rieffe, C.; Crone, E. A.

downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not infer stable prosocial dispositions or universal effects from a short experimental task.

title: Peer Influence on Risk Taking, Risk Preference, and Risky Decision Making in Adolescence and Adulthood

uncertainty_description: The relationship between simulated risk effects and ordinary adolescent behaviour is uncertain.

downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not generalise peer-presence effects to all peers, decisions, adolescents or real-world risks.

title: Age Differences in Resistance to Peer Influence

downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review

title: Preference and Popularity as Distinct Forms of Status: A Meta-Analytic Review of 20 Years of Research

source_author_or_org: van den Berg, Y. H. M.; Lansu, T. A. M.; Cillessen, A. H. N.

downstream_use: Stage 1 synthesis subject to Gate 2 review
prohibited_overextension: Do not equate popularity with acceptance, friendship, wellbeing or prosocial standing.

title: Avoiding Social Risk in Adolescence
description: Avoiding exclusion and reputational loss may organise adolescent choices alongside reward seeking.

title: The Need to Contribute During Adolescence

title: What Schools Need to Know About Fostering School Belonging: A Meta-Analysis

prohibited_overextension: Do not treat correlates as proven intervention levers or assume one universal model of belonging.

title: The Relationships Between School Belonging and Students' Motivational, Social-Emotional, Behavioural, and Academic Outcomes in Secondary Education: A Meta-Analytic Review
description: Belonging was associated with motivation, adjustment, behaviour and academic outcomes.

prohibited_overextension: Do not infer that belonging alone causes academic, behavioural or wellbeing outcomes.

description: Connectedness was associated with lower mental-health, substance-use, violence and sexual-health risks.
